events.title: Bloody Sunday, Edmund Pettus Bridge
events.blurb: The march from Selma toward Montgomery on March 7, 1965, halted at the foot of the Edmund Pettus Bridge by Alabama state troopers. Witnesses, organizers, and marchers describe what they saw.

events.title: Greensboro Sit-Ins
events.blurb: Four North Carolina A&T students refused to leave a Woolworth lunch counter on February 1, 1960. The action ignited a wave of sit-ins across the South within weeks.

events.title: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom
events.blurb: Some 250,000 people gathered at the Lincoln Memorial on August 28, 1963, behind a coalition of civil-rights, labor, and religious organizations. Witnesses describe the scale, the organizing, and the rhetoric.

events.title: 16th Street Baptist Church Bombing
events.blurb: A Ku Klux Klan bomb killed four Black girls and injured many at the 16th Street Baptist Church in Birmingham, Alabama, on September 15, 1963. Interviewees recall the day and its aftermath.

events.title: Freedom Summer
events.blurb: A coalition of SNCC, CORE, NAACP, and SCLC organizers brought hundreds of mostly-white northern student volunteers into Mississippi for the summer of 1964 to register voters and staff Freedom Schools.

events.title: Dr. King Assassination
events.blurb: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was shot at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis on April 4, 1968. Movement contemporaries describe where they were and what they did in the hours and days after.

events.title: Voting Rights Act of 1965
events.blurb: Lyndon Johnson signed the Voting Rights Act on August 6, 1965, after the Selma campaign forced the federal government to act. Organizers describe what the legislation changed on the ground.

events.title: Emmett Till
events.blurb: The fourteen-year-old Chicago boy abducted from his great-uncle's home in Money, Mississippi on August 28, 1955, and murdered. Wheeler Parker Jr. is the last living witness; he and others describe the events and what followed.
